User Stories from a Frontend Dev's Perspective
I touched on User Stories in an earlier post, but recently ran through them for real on a project — and the mindset is genuinely different from what I was used to.
User Stories from a frontend angle

A while back I shipped a small project with my team, and User Stories really challenged how I'd usually plan systems. I used to start from the feature side — "this feature needs these capabilities, these database columns, can do this and that..."
After actually using User Story planning though, I realized it's the inverse. User Story = user's story. The lens isn't "what features do we need?", it's "
". You don't start with features — you start with what the user needs to do.
Using a job board as the example, here's how it played out:
A User Story is usually a single sentence — then you discuss outward from it:
Job seekers need a place to post resumes
From that one line, brainstorm what features a job seeker would actually need:
Opt out from being found by specific industries
(Honestly, the first time you do this it's hard. You don't naturally think of half of these.)
Then drill into one of the features — for resume, what fields are needed?
Fill out resume → toggle resume visibility on → expose to job postings
In contrast, backend planning has a different mental model.
Start by asking what features you need:
Then define types for each column. (Honestly been a while since I've planned a database, so apologies if details are rusty.)
That's the gist. I'm still getting comfortable with the User Story way of working, so stopping here for now.

A follow-up after running through User Stories on a real project — the mindset really is opposite to traditional backend planning. Backend devs typically start with "what features and database columns do we need?" but User Stories start with "what does the user need?". This post is my notes from the first time I lived through that shift on a job-board example.
